elements.sent-2411-001: This is, for example, useful when one wants to understand the types of spacetime geometry that can be realised in general relativity.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, it is currently unknown what (if any) energy condition a realistic quantum theory interacting gravitationally would obey.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this thesis, we provide support for the conformally invariant averaged null energy condition (CANEC), a particular proposal towards a more generally valid energy condition.
elements.sent-2411-004: We motivate our interest in the CANEC by reviewing the role of the null energy condition (NEC) in classical gravity, summarising a general proof of the averaged NEC (ANEC) for quantum field theories in flat spacetime, and going over a holographic proof of the CANEC in a class of curved spacetimes.
elements.sent-2411-005: The context in which the CANEC applies is then extended with two proofs: a holographic proof in two types of spacetime to which the existing proof does not apply, and a field theoretical proof for conformal field theories in conformally flat spacetimes.
elements.sent-2411-006: Finally, we consider light-ray operators in Minkowski spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-007: The connection between these operators and the CANEC is that the CANEC on a lightcone in Minkowski spacetime constrains a light-ray operator for an even number of spacetime dimensions $d$.
elements.sent-2411-008: Via an explicit computation, we show that many light-ray operators are positive-semidefinite in a class of non-minimally coupled but otherwise free real scalar field theories.
elements.sent-2411-009: One of the operators for which this holds is the CANEC-operator on the lightcone for even $d$.. \hypersetup{ linkcolor=blue, \mainmatter.}
elements.sent-2411-010: General relativity describes gravity as a feature of the geometry of spacetime: absent other forces, matter and radiation move on geodesics in a curved spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-011: This curvature is in turn caused by matter and radiation, a relationship summarised in Einstein's field equations \cite{Einstein,HawkingEllis,Carroll_2019 : \begin{equation}R_{ab} - \frac{1}{2}g_{ab}R = 8\pi G_N T_{ab}\ , \label{H1_EFE}\end{equation} The terms on the left-hand side (collectively known as the Einstein tensor) depend only on the metric tensor $g_{ab}$, which describes the geometry of spacetime; the energy-momentum tensor $T_{ab}$ on the right-hand side describes the matter and radiation in this spacetime in terms of quantities like the energy density and the pressure.}
elements.sent-2411-012: When solving \eqref{H1_EFE , a typical approach is to postulate an energy-momentum tensor (or calculate $T_{ab}$ for a given matter distribution) and solve for $g_{ab}$ to find the geometry associated with the postulated matter configuration.}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, the inverse is also possible: fix a metric and use \eqref{H1_EFE to find the $T_{ab}$ needed to support it.}
elements.sent-2411-014: General relativity thus allows, in principle, for any spacetime geometry, including those with exotic features such as wormholes, closed timelike curves, and faster-than-light signalling.
elements.sent-2411-015: The fact that we have not (yet) observed these phenomena in nature raises the question: are these exotic phenomena physically realistic but for some reason hard to find, or are they merely mathematical artefacts? A related question can be asked with regard to black holes.
elements.sent-2411-016: It is well-known that the simplest black hole geometry (Schwarzschild spacetime) contains a point where the curvature diverges; similarly, explicit calculations have shown that the spherically symmetric collapse of a sufficiently large amount of pressureless matter leads to a divergent density \cite{Oppenheimer_1939 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Again, one may ask whether these singularities are physically relevant or artefacts of the high degree of symmetry in these scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-018: Energy conditions provide a way to answer these questions (see \cite{Kontou_review for a recent review).}
elements.sent-2411-019: They are attempts to summarise the properties of realistic matter, usually expressed as an inequality that the energy-momentum tensor of a realistic theory satisfies.
elements.sent-2411-020: Examples of the properties encoded by energy conditions are the notion that realistic matter has a positive energy density or, through \eqref{H1_EFE , that it gravitationally focuses light-rays.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Such a constraint is often sufficient to exclude exotic spacetimes \cite{Olum_1998,AANEC , which are supported by unrealistic matter (e.g. wormholes usually require some negative energy, but note \cite{wormholes_ANEC ).}}
elements.sent-2411-022: The role of energy conditions in the study of singularities is most evident in the various singularity theorems \cite{Senovilla_1998 , for example the one formulated by Penrose \cite{Penrose_1965 .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: To state his theorem, Penrose introduced the concept of a trapped surface: a $d-2$ dimensional spacelike surface in a $d$ dimensional spacetime such that all light-rays emitted orthogonally from it initially converge.
elements.sent-2411-024: If the matter in the spacetime is then unable to defocus null geodesics (a property which can be expressed as an energy condition), Penrose showed that the spacetime must contain null geodesics that encounter an edge of spacetime, i.e. they are geodesically incomplete.
elements.sent-2411-025: In the context of a gravitationally collapsing matter distribution, this incompleteness is usually interpreted as indicating the presence of a singularity \cite{HawkingEllis .}
elements.sent-2411-026: These are all results of classical general relativity.
elements.sent-2411-027: However, real matter is not classical; it is described by quantum field theories (QFTs), which allow an arbitrarily negative expectation value $\mean{T_{ab _\psi$ at any single point in spacetime \cite{EGJ_NietPuntsgewijs .}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: This has spurred the development of averaged energy conditions, which usually consider some complete causal (i.e. null or timelike) geodesic, contract the energy-momentum tensor with its tangent vector, and integrate the expectation value of the result along the geodesic.
elements.sent-2411-029: The most prominent of these averaged energy conditions is the averaged null energy condition (ANEC), which states that for any state $\ket{\psi}$ \begin{equation}\int_\gamma\mean{T_{ab}}_\psi l^a l^b\,\d\lambda \geq 0\ , \label{scr_ANEC}\end{equation} where $\gamma$ is a complete null geodesic with tangent vector $l^a$ and affine parameter $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-030: The ANEC can be proven to hold for general QFTs in Minkowski spacetime, using quantum information techniques (specifically the monotonicity of relative entropy) \cite{ANEC_Entropy or an argument based on causality \cite{ANEC_causaliteit .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: However, these arguments no longer hold in curved spacetimes, and the ANEC can be violated in e.g. Schwarzschild spacetime \cite{ANECviolation_Schwarzschild or conformally flat spacetimes \cite{ANEC_violation_old,ANEC_violation_new .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: This violation opens up the possibility that exotic spacetimes could be viable once quantum effects are taken into account, and that these same quantum effects might prevent gravitational collapse from forming a singularity.
elements.sent-2411-033: To study this possibility, one may attempt to find an energy condition for QFTs that continues to hold in curved spacetimes.
elements.sent-2411-034: Several proposals have been made to generalise \eqref{scr_ANEC to curved spacetimes, the most well-known being the self-consistent, achronal ANEC (AANEC) \cite{AANEC .}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The AANEC posits that \eqref{scr_ANEC holds in a curved spacetime if $\gamma$ is a complete achronal null geodesic (i.e. $\lambda$ is unbounded, and $\gamma$ does not connect timelike separated points), and the spacetime is semi-classically self-consistent, obeying \cite{Rosenfeld_1963 \begin{equation}R_{ab} - \frac{1}{2}g_{ab}R = 8\pi G_N\mean{T_{ab}}_\psi\ . \label{H1_SemiclassEFE}\end{equation} Another approach is to first formulate an energy condition for QFTs in fixed spacetimes, in which the quantum fields do not affect the geometry.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: An indication for the existence of such energy conditions is provided by quantum energy inequalities \cite{Fewster_2012 , which are constraints similar to \eqref{scr_ANEC but with a negative (possibly state-dependent) lower bound, and can be proven to hold for specific QFTs on spacetimes with a fixed geometry.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: One can also attempt to make the ANEC more local, for example by introducing a weighting function (see e.g. \cite{QEI_2D,DSNEC_scalar,DSNEC_fermion for examples in flat spacetime); an important instance of such an energy condition in curved spacetimes is the conformally invariant ANEC (CANEC) \cite{CANEC_odd,CANEC_even : \begin{equation}\int_{\lambda_-}^{\lambda_+}\eta^d\mean{T_{ab}}_\psi l^al^b\d\lambda \geq B\ . \label{intro_CANEC}\end{equation} For the CANEC, we consider a small bundle of null geodesics in a $d$ dimensional spacetime and integrate along one of them, with affine parameter $\lambda$ and tangent vector $l^a$.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: The weighting function $\eta$ is the Jacobi field, which measures the distance between neighbouring geodesics in the bundle and vanishes at $\lambda = \lambda_\pm$; the bound $B$ vanishes for odd $d$, but is non-trivial for even $d$ due to the conformal anomaly.
elements.sent-2411-039: The goal of this thesis is twofold.
elements.sent-2411-040: On the one hand, we provide support for the CANEC as a generalisation of the classical null energy condition to quantum fields in curved spacetimes by extending the context in which it applies.
elements.sent-2411-041: On the other hand, we will demonstrate that certain light-ray operators \cite{LO_original,LO_Mathys only have non-negative expectation values for a non-minimally coupled real scalar field in flat spacetime.}
elements.sent-2411-042: These two topics are connected by the observation that for even $d$, there is a light-ray operator which corresponds to the CANEC-operator on the Minkowski spacetime lightcone.
elements.sent-2411-043: In light of these aims we review several results from the literature that motivate our interest in the null energy, in its average value, and finally in the CANEC itself.
elements.sent-2411-044: Furthermore, we do several novel computations, of which we would like to highlight three results.
elements.sent-2411-045: The original proof of the CANEC \cite{CANEC_odd,CANEC_even considered holographic CFTs (conformally invariant QFTs which, by the AdS/CFT correspondence, also describe semi-classical gravity) in a specific class of spacetimes.}
elements.sent-2411-046: We demonstrate that this class can be somewhat extended in two ways.
elements.sent-2411-047: For $d=3$, we use Gaussian normal coordinates to show that the steps in the proof of \cite{CANEC_odd are valid in a general spacetime, although they only lead to a non-trivial energy condition in spacetimes that satisfy a geometrical constraint.}
elements.sent-2411-048: This constraint is violated by maximally symmetric and Ricci flat spacetimes, but we nevertheless find that holographic CFTs on (a subset of) these spacetimes obey an energy condition which either is the CANEC or closely related to it.
elements.sent-2411-049: For this proof, we follow the method of \cite{holographic_ANEC , and our result in maximally symmetric spacetimes essentially supplements the results of \cite{holographic_ANEC and \cite{Rosso_2020 with a proof in AdS spacetime.}}}
elements.paragraph-2411-002: Field theoretical proof in conformally flat spacetimes.
elements.sent-2411-050: Since holographic CFTs are rather special, we also consider more general CFTs.
elements.sent-2411-051: Following up on \cite{Rosso_2020 , we show that for general CFTs, the Weyl transformation of the Minkowski spacetime ANEC can be interpreted as the CANEC in a conformally flat spacetime.}
elements.sent-2411-052: To ensure that this Weyl transformation makes sense, we demand that the conformal factor is positive and finite everywhere except possibly at the edges of the flat and conformally flat spacetimes; it must also be positive and finite along a complete null geodesic in Minkowski spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-053: The CFT is constrained by the demand that the fields are smooth in both the flat and conformally flat spacetimes, which implies that it should be possible to impose Dirichlet boundary conditions on the CFT.
elements.sent-2411-054: The aforementioned proof of the CANEC for general CFTs relies on the fact that the Minkowski spacetime ANEC can be interpreted as the CANEC on a null plane.
elements.sent-2411-055: Inspired by the possibility of a CANEC on more general null congruences in Minkowski spacetime, we turn to a set of related objects: light-ray operators \eqref{LO_def \cite{LO_original,LO_Mathys .}}
elements.sent-2411-056: We study them for a non-minimally coupled real scalar field in Minkowski spacetime and find that the light-ray operators which satisfy \eqref{LO_NonMinConstraint only have non-negative expectation values.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Although this provides a large number of constraints on the theory, we argue that it does not generally imply that the CANEC holds on arbitrary null congruences in Minkowski spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-058: Chapter \ref{chap_ClassEC first motivates our interest in the null energy by discussing several classical energy conditions and proving Penrose's singularity theorem from the null energy condition.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Then, chapter \ref{chap_ANEC demonstrates the failure of classical energy conditions in QFT, sketches the proof of ANEC for QFTs in Minkowski spacetime from \cite{ANEC_Entropy , and briefly describes the ANEC's failure in fixed curved spacetimes.}}
elements.sent-2411-060: This motivates a search for generalisations of the ANEC to curved spacetimes, two of which are discussed and, to some degree, proven in chapter \ref{chap_curveANEC : the CANEC, following \cite{CANEC_odd , and the AANEC, following \cite{Wall .}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: We expand on the former in chapter \ref{chap_BeyondCANEC , which aims to extend the CANEC to background spacetimes and CFTs which are not covered by the proof in chapter \ref{chap_curveANEC .}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Inspired by the discussion in chapter \ref{chap_BeyondCANEC , chapter \ref{chap_LO discusses light-ray operators for the non-minimally coupled but otherwise free real scalar field and demonstrates that many of them only have non-negative expectation values.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Finally, we summarise our results and discuss possible future research in chapter \ref{chap_Conc .}
elements.sent-2411-064: The rest of this introduction establishes our notation and conventions; a brief introduction of relevant concepts from general relativity and QFT can be found in appendices \ref{app_GR and \ref{app_QFT respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: In this thesis, we will employ the abstract index notation from \cite{abstracte_indices , in which Latin indices from the beginning of the alphabet indicate how many (co-)vectors a tensor can act upon; tensor operations are denoted in the way that is familiar from the usual index notation.}
elements.sent-2411-066: For example, $R^a_{\ \,bcd}$ is a $(1,3)$-tensor; its components with respect to some explicit basis of (co-)vectors are $R^\mu_{\ \,\nu\rho\sigma}$, and acting on a vector $l^a$ with $R^a_{\ \,bcd}$ is denoted as e.g. $R^a_{\ \,bcd l^b$.}
elements.sent-2411-067: For a covector acting on a vector (and vice versa) we will also use a dot, e.g. $p\cdot x = p_ax^a$.
elements.sent-2411-068: The main benefit of abstract index notation is that it makes coordinate independence more manifest.
elements.sent-2411-069: We define a spacetime as a connected $d$ dimensional manifold $M$ equipped with a smooth, symmetric metric tensor $g_{ab}$ of Lorentzian signature; we also demand that $M$ is time-orientable, so one can consistently assign a sense of `past' and `future' to any point in $M$.
elements.sent-2411-070: We use the mostly plus sign convention, so that the Lorentzian signature implies that the matrix of metric components $(g_{\mu\nu )$ has one negative eigenvalue.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Due to this negative eigenvalue, the norm $g_{ab X^aX^b$ of a vector $X^a$ can be positive, zero, or negative, which classifies $X^a$ as respectively spacelike, null, or timelike; null and timelike vectors will also be referred to as `causal', as explained in section \ref{sec_Causality .}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Similarly, we classify a submanifold $N\subseteq M$ based on its normal vector: if $N$ has an everywhere timelike, spacelike, or null normal vector, $N$ is referred to as spacelike, timelike, or null respectively.
elements.sent-2411-073: If $N$ is $d-n$ dimensional, we say that it has codimension $n$; a codimension 1 submanifold is also known as a hypersurface.
elements.sent-2411-074: For the covariant derivative, we make use of the Levi-Civita connection $\Gamma^\mu_{\rho\sigma}$.
elements.sent-2411-075: The Riemann tensor, which measures the local curvature, is defined as \begin{equation}[\nabla_c,\nabla_d]X^a \alis R^a_{\ \,bcd}X^b\ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-077: Given an action $S_\mathrm{mat}$ for the fields describing matter, we define the energy-momentum tensor as \begin{equation}T_{ab} = -\frac{2}{\sqrt{-g}}\funci{S_mat}{g^{ab}}\ ,\end{equation} where $g = \mathrm{det (g_{\mu\nu )$.}}

elements.sent-2411-084: In Minkowski spacetime we usually consider null geodesics with $x^+ = \lambda$ and constant $x^-$ and $\ve{x ^\perp$, where $\lambda$ is the affine parameter.}
elements.sent-2411-085: In this chapter, we review some energy conditions in the context of classical general relativity in order to motivate our later focus on the averaged null energy condition.
elements.sent-2411-086: To facilitate our discussion, section \ref{sec_Causality introduces several concepts related to causality in general relativity based on the treatments of \cite{HawkingEllis and \cite{Witten_causality .}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: In section \ref{sec_classEC , we discuss four important energy conditions, before concluding this chapter with a brief review of one of their most important consequences, Penrose's singularity theorem, in section \ref{sec_Penrose .}}
elements.sent-2411-088: The goal of much work in physics is to formulate and solve initial value problems: given initial data about a physical system, what will the state of the system be at some point in its future? The amount of initial data required to make this prediction is limited by the causal structure of spacetime, which this section introduces briefly based on \cite{HawkingEllis and \cite{Witten_causality .}}
elements.sent-2411-089: In a spacetime $M$, the amount of information required to predict the future state of a system from initial data is restricted by the postulate of local causality: if $\U\subseteq M$ is a convex normal neighbourhood (i.e. any two points in \U\ are connected by a unique geodesic $\gamma\subset\U$), then a causal signal can only be sent between $p,q\in\U$ if there exists a curve between them with an everywhere either timelike or null tangent vector.
elements.sent-2411-090: Such a curve is called causal.
elements.sent-2411-091: It follows that events at $p\in M$ only affect events at $q\in M$ if $q$ can be reached from $p$ with a future-directed causal curve; after all, any curve connecting $p$ to $q$ can be covered with convex normal neighbourhoods and should be causal in each of these neighbourhoods to allow a signal to propagate along it.
elements.sent-2411-092: This leads us to define the causal future of a subset $W\subset M$ as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-093: The causal future of $W$, denoted $J^+(W)$, is the set of points that can be reached from at least one point in $W$ by a future-directed causal curve; we let $W\subset J^+(W)$.
elements.sent-2411-094: The boundary of $J^+(W)$, denoted $\partial J^+(W)$, is formed by those $p\in J^+(W)$ for which no open region $\U\subset J^+(W)$ with $p\in\U$ exists.
elements.sent-2411-095: The causal past of $W$, denoted $J^-(W)$, is defined similarly by replacing `future' by `past'.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Prompt curves and the boundary of the causal future
elements.sent-2411-096: Closely tied to the boundary of the causal future is the notion of promptness.
elements.sent-2411-097: A curve from a subset $W\subset M$ to any $p\in M$ is called prompt if it is future-directed and causal, and there are no future-directed causal curves from $W$ to any point in the interior of $J^-(p)$.
elements.sent-2411-098: Thus, if $p\in\partial J^+(W)$, all causal curves from $W$ to $p$ are prompt; if they weren't, then either $p\notin J^+(W)$, contradicting $p\in\partial J^+(W)$, or there would be an $r\in J^+(W)$ with $p$ in the interior of $J^+(r)$, implying the existence of an open region $\U\subset J^+(r)\subseteq J^+(W)$ containing $p$, also contradicting $p\in\partial J^+(W)$.
elements.sent-2411-099: Conversely, if a prompt curve from $W$ to $q\in M$ exists, then $q\in J^+(W)$ since prompt curves are causal and future-directed; furthermore, any open region $\U\subset M$ containing $q$ has a non-empty intersection with the interior of $J^-(q)$, implying that $\U\cap J^+(W)\neq\U$ since the curve from $W$ to $q$ is prompt.
elements.sent-2411-100: Hence, there is no open $\U\subset J^+(W)$ which contains $q$, so $q\in\partial J^+(W)$.
elements.sent-2411-101: In summary, $p\in \partial J^+(W)$ if and only if there is a prompt curve from $W$ to $p$.
elements.sent-2411-102: A curve $\gamma$ starts out as a null geodesic (drawn as a solid line), has a timelike segment around some point $p$ (marked by a dotted line) and then continues as a null geodesic (represented with a solid line).
elements.sent-2411-103: The geometry near $p$ can be approximated by Minkowski spacetime, meaning that we can start a null geodesic $\gamma'$, drawn as a dashed line, from somewhere on the timelike segment of $\gamma$ and have it stay to the past of $\gamma$ everywhere.
elements.sent-2411-104: For example, they are (segments of) null geodesics.
elements.sent-2411-105: That they are null curves follows from considering a causal curve $\gamma$ which is timelike near $p\in\gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-106: There exists a neighbourhood \U\ of $p$ where the metric is well-approximated by the Minkowski metric\footnote{Following \cite{Carroll_2019 , this can be seen in the Riemann normal coordinates introduced at the end of section \ref{sec_GeodCurv .}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: These are defined in a normal neighbourhood $\N_p$ of $p$ and allow the coordinates along any geodesic to be parametrised as $x^\mu(\lambda) = \lambda k^\mu$ for constant $k^\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-108: Through the geodesic equation \eqref{AppA_geodeet with $V^\mu = X^\mu = k^\mu$, we find that $\Gamma^\rho_{\mu\nu = 0$ at $p$, which by metric compatibility means that $\partial_\sigma g_{\mu\nu = 0$ at $p$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Since we can choose the basis vectors such that $g_{\mu\nu = \eta_{\mu\nu}$ at $p$, there must be some neighbourhood of $p$ that is well-described by Minkowski spacetime.}
elements.sent-2411-111: That a prompt curve should be a geodesic follows by considering a null curve $\gamma$ which has a bend at $p\in\gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-112: By again looking at a neighbourhood \U\ of $p$ in which the metric is well-approximated by the Minkowski metric, we find that a null curve $\gamma'$ exists which initially coincides with $\gamma$, makes the same bend as $\gamma$ but earlier, and is afterwards to the past of $\gamma$, meaning that $\gamma$ was not prompt.
elements.sent-2411-113: The above reasoning suggests another necessary property of a prompt curve starting at a set of points $W$: it cannot contain a caustic, defined as a point that can be reached from $W$ via two distinct null geodesics (if we have a null congruence emanating from $W$, a caustic can be recognised as a point where the Jacobi field $\eta$ vanishes).
elements.sent-2411-114: After all, if some $p\in M$ could be reached from $W$ via the null geodesics $\gamma_1$ and $\gamma_2$, a third null curve $\gamma_3$ could be constructed by combining the section of $\gamma_1$ between $W$ and $p$ with the section of $\gamma_2$ beyond $p$.
elements.sent-2411-115: A third property of prompt curves is that a null geodesic $\gamma$ from a submanifold $W$ to a point $p$ can only be prompt if it intersects $W$ at a right angle.
elements.sent-2411-116: If it didn't, there would be another curve $\gamma'$ which comes closer to intersecting $W$ at a right angle and hence would need to traverse less spatial distance to reach $p$.
elements.sent-2411-117: Also note that a null geodesic can only be perpendicular to submanifolds with a codimension of at least 2.
elements.sent-2411-118: Finally, the fact that $\partial J^+(W)$ is spanned by prompt curves allows us to conclude that $\partial J^+(W)$ is an achronal hypersurface, i.e. a hypersurface that does not contain timelike separated points.
elements.sent-2411-119: First, consider that any prompt curve must be achronal.
elements.sent-2411-120: After all, if a null geodesic $\gamma$ is not achronal, there exist timelike separated $p,q\in\gamma$ and we can construct a new curve $\gamma'$ by replacing $\gamma_{pq}$ (the part of $\gamma$ connecting $p$ and $q$) with a timelike curve.
elements.sent-2411-121: Since $\gamma'$ is not a null geodesic, it is not prompt, and because $\gamma'$ coincides with $\gamma$ outside of $\gamma_{pq}$, we conclude that $\gamma$ is not prompt if it is not achronal.
elements.sent-2411-122: Because $\partial J^+(W)$ is spanned by prompt curves, this implies that $\partial J^+(W)$ is achronal.
elements.sent-2411-123: We prove that $\partial J^+(W)$ is also a hypersurface by choosing any $p\in\partial J^+(W)$ and considering the convex normal neighbourhood $\N_p$ of $p$.
elements.sent-2411-124: In $\N_p$, we choose coordinates $x^\mu$ (with $x^i$ for $i\in\cuha{1,\ldots,d-1}$ the spacelike coordinates) such that the curves $\gamma(\ve{a ) = \cuha{q\in\N_p:x^i(q) = a^i}$ intersect the interiors of both $J^+(p)$ and $J^-(p)$.}

elements.sent-2411-126: The point $r$ where this happens is uniquely labelled by $\ve{a}$, so the map $\phi_p:\N_p\cap\partial J^+(W) \rightarrow \mathbb{R ^{d-1}$ with $\phi_p(r) = \ve{a}$ is injective.}
elements.sent-2411-127: The combination $(\phi_p,\N_p)$ therefore defines a chart, which can be extended to an atlas of $\partial J^+(W)$ by repeating the above process at sufficiently many $p\in\partial J^+(W)$.
elements.sent-2411-128: If $M$ has no edge, $\partial J^+(W)$ has no edge either (if $\partial J^+(W)$ had an edge $\Gamma$, $\partial J^+(W)$ could be extended with the relevant part of $\partial J^+(\Gamma)$), so we conclude that $\partial J^+(W)$ is indeed an achronal codimension 1 submanifold or hypersurface.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Initial value surfaces and globally hyperbolic spacetimes
elements.sent-2411-129: Having studied the boundary of the causal future in some detail, we now return to the relation between the causal structure of spacetime and initial value problems.
elements.sent-2411-130: Let us assume that we specify initial data for some theory on a subset $\Sigma\subset M$.
elements.sent-2411-131: If we can then predict what happens at $p\in J^+(\Sigma)$ based on this data, there should be no past-inextendible causal curves (causal curves that either stretch into the asymptotic past or originate at the past edge of spacetime) through $p$ that did not pass $\Sigma$ at some point, since these would carry influences that we cannot predict based on the data on $\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-132: This leads us to define the domain of dependence:.
elements.sent-2411-133: The domain of dependence of a subset $\Sigma\subset M$, denoted $D(\Sigma)$, is the set of all points $p\in M$ such that every inextendible causal curve through $p$ intersects with $\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-134: The edge of $D(\Sigma)$, denoted $\partial D(\Sigma)$, is called the Cauchy horizon of $\Sigma$; it too can be divided into a past and future Cauchy horizon, respectively $\partial D^-(\Sigma) = \partial D(\Sigma)\cap J^-(\Sigma)$ and $\partial D^+(\Sigma) = \partial D(\Sigma)\cap J^+(\Sigma)$.
elements.sent-2411-135: Note that by this definition, $\Sigma\subset D(\Sigma)$.
elements.sent-2411-136: Any $\Sigma$ has a domain of dependence, but formulating an initial value problem calls for a relativistic analogue to the Newtonian notion of an instant of time at which one defines the initial data.
elements.sent-2411-137: We will thus always define initial data on a $\Sigma$ that is a spacelike, achronal hypersurface; such a $\Sigma$ will be called an initial value hypersurface.
elements.sent-2411-138: The rationale behind defining initial data only on spacelike achronal hypersurfaces is that they do not contain points that can be connected by a causal curve, guaranteeing that the initial data cannot influence itself.
elements.sent-2411-139: If one is interested in the global properties of spacetime, it becomes relevant to consider whether all physical processes in a spacetime could be formulated as an initial value problem; that is, whether there exists an initial value hypersurface $\Sigma$ such that $D(\Sigma) = M$.
elements.sent-2411-140: As both \cite{HawkingEllis and \cite{Witten_causality point out, there are no obvious grounds to assume this is the case in the actual universe and there are known solutions to general relativity that do not allow for such an initial value hypersurface (most notably the anti-de Sitter and Reissner-NordstrĂ¶m solutions); however, treating physical calculations as initial value problems has historically worked quite well, and we have no indications yet that the universe as a whole will deviate from that trend.}}
elements.sent-2411-141: We therefore assume that the universe is globally hyperbolic, which we define as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-142: If $D(\Sigma) = M$, $\Sigma$ is called a Cauchy surface, and a spacetime which contains a Cauchy surface is called globally hyperbolic.
elements.sent-2411-143: A useful fact about Cauchy surfaces is that any achronal hypersurface $S$ (which may be another Cauchy surface) can be mapped onto them injectively, because one can construct a future-directed timelike vector field (the following construction comes from \cite{Witten_causality ).}
elements.sent-2411-144: This can be done by choosing a coordinate system for an open region containing any $p\in M$ and finding an eigenvector $V^a$ of $\roha{g_{\mu\nu }$ at $p$ with a negative eigenvalue.}
elements.sent-2411-145: Repeating this process at all points in $M$ extends $V^a$ to a future-directed timelike vector field, which can act as the tangent vector field for a set of inextendible curves known as the integral curves of $V^a$.
elements.sent-2411-146: These curves are everywhere timelike and hence intersect any achronal hypersurface at most once, and a Cauchy surface exactly once.
elements.sent-2411-147: Thus, given an achronal hypersurface $S$ and a Cauchy surface $\Sigma$, we can define a map $\varphi:S\rightarrow\Sigma$ which sends $p\in S$ to the point $q\in\Sigma$ where the integral curve through $p$ intersects $\Sigma$.
elements.section-2411-004: Classical energy conditions and their interpretation
elements.sent-2411-148: As described in chapter \ref{chap_Intro , energy conditions are often conceived of as constraints on the energy-momentum tensor $T_{ab}$ of a gravitating system, imposed to rule out geometries with pathological features.}
elements.sent-2411-149: The simplest way to formulate such a constraint is as a pointwise inequality, providing a bound on some component of $T_{ab}$ at every point in spacetime; because we are usually interested in gravitating systems, we also impose \eqref{H1_EFE , which translates a bound on $T_{ab}$ to a bound on $R_{ab}$, $R$, and $g_{ab}$.}
elements.sent-2411-150: Four commonly used energy conditions are:.
elements.sent-2411-151: The weak energy condition (WEC), which states that for any timelike vector $t^a$ \begin{equation}T_{ab}t^at^b \geq 0\ . \label{classEC_WEC}\end{equation} The equivalent constraint on geometric quantities is straightforward to read off from \eqref{H1_EFE , but due to difficulties in interpreting it \cite{Kontou_review , it is not often used.}}
elements.sent-2411-152: The strong energy condition (SEC), according to which for any timelike $t^a$ \begin{equation}\roha{T_{ab} - \frac{T^c_{\ \,c}}{d-2}g_{ab}}t^at^b \geq 0\ . \label{classEC_SEC}\end{equation} For the SEC, we will see that it is actually the geometric constraint $R_{ab t^at^b\geq0$ which allows a more straightforward interpretation.}
elements.sent-2411-153: The dominant energy condition (DEC) requires that, for any future-directed, timelike $t^a$, $-T^{ab t_a$ is future-directed and causal (i.e. timelike or null).}
elements.sent-2411-154: In turn, this means that for any two future-directed timelike vectors $t^a$ and $p^a$, \begin{equation}T_{ab}t^ap^b \geq 0\ . \label{classEC_DEC}\end{equation} Similarly to the WEC, the geometric bound this implies through \eqref{H1_EFE is straightforward to read off, but difficult to interpret and hence rarely used.}
elements.sent-2411-155: The null energy condition (NEC) claims that for any null vector $l^a$ we must have \begin{equation}T_{ab}l^al^b\geq0\ . \label{classEC_NEC}\end{equation} As with the SEC, the geometric version $R_{ab l^al^b\geq 0$ will turn out to be easier to interpret.}
elements.sent-2411-156: Before interpreting these energy conditions, we use a continuity argument from \cite{Kontou_review to demonstrate that the NEC is implied by the other three conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-157: We begin by observing that the WEC, SEC, and DEC can be written as $S_{ab p^aq^b\geq0$ for co-oriented timelike $p^a$ and $q^a$ and a symmetric tensor $S_{ab}$.}
elements.sent-2411-158: Let us consider a weakened statement, namely $S_{ab p^aq^b\geq B$ for some fixed $B\inR$ and all $p^a$ and $q^a$ with $p_ap^a = q_aq^a = -1$ (without the restriction to unit vectors we could arbitrarily rescale $p^a$ and $q^a$, so that the bound would only hold for the rescaled vectors if $B\geq0$, which doesn't weaken the original statement).}
elements.sent-2411-159: We then choose two null vectors $l^a$ and $k^a$ with $l_ak^a = -\frac{1 {2}$, and construct two timelike unit vectors from them: \begin{equation}p^a = \frac{1}{r}l^a + rk^a and q^a = \frac{1}{s}l^a + sk^a\ ,\end{equation} for any $r,s>0$.}
elements.sent-2411-160: With these choices of $p^a$ and $q^a$, the bound on $S_{ab}$ implies \begin{equation}S_{ab}p^aq^b = \frac{1}{rs}S_{ab}l^al^b + \roha{\frac{s}{r}l^ak^b + \frac{r}{s}k^al^b}S_{ab} + rsS_{ab}k^ak^b \geq \ B \non S_{ab}l^al^b + \roha{s^2l^ak^b + r^2k^al^b}S_{ab} + r^2s^2S_{ab}k^ak^b \geq \ rsB\ .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}S_{ab}p^aq^b = \frac{1}{rs}S_{ab}l^al^b + \roha{\frac{s}{r}l^ak^b + \frac{r}{s}k^al^b}S_{ab} + rsS_{ab}k^ak^b \geq \ B \non S_{ab}l^al^b + \roha{s^2l^ak^b + r^2k^al^b}S_{ab} + r^2s^2S_{ab}k^ak^b \geq \ rsB\ .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-161: From the limit $r,s\downarrow0$ we see that $S_{ab p^aq^b\geq B$ implies $S_{ab l^al^b\geq0$ for any null vector $l^a$.}}
elements.sent-2411-162: For the WEC, SEC, and DEC we see that $S_{ab l^al^b = T_{ab l^al^b$, so all three conditions imply the NEC.}}
elements.sent-2411-163: The inverse obviously does not hold.
elements.sent-2411-164: Let us now give a physical interpretation of the four energy conditions.
elements.sent-2411-165: For the WEC and DEC, this is relatively straightforward, as we can interpret them directly in terms of the energy-momentum tensor.
elements.sent-2411-166: The WEC simply states that any observer, travelling on a timelike curve with tangent vector $t^a$, must measure a positive energy density \cite{Kontou_review .}
elements.sent-2411-167: Note however \cite{Curiel2017 , which points out that negative energies arise quite naturally in the ergosphere of Kerr black holes \cite{Carroll_2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-168: However, these energies are defined through the Killing vectors of the Kerr metric, and hence are not the energies any observer would naturally measure; nevertheless, they complicate the simplest interpretation of the WEC.
elements.sent-2411-169: The statement of the DEC is that an observer on a timelike curve with tangent vector $t^a$ must always observe a causal flux $-T^{ab t_a$ of energy-momentum \cite{Kontou_review .}}
elements.sent-2411-170: This is sometimes said to imply that energy-momentum can only propagate causally (e.g. in \cite{HawkingEllis,Senovilla_1998,QEI_2D ), supported by a theorem which states that if a covariantly conserved $T_{ab}$ obeying the DEC vanishes on a closed, achronal set, then it must vanish on the future domain of dependence of this set \cite{HawkingEllis .}}
elements.sent-2411-171: However, as shown in \cite{Earman_2014 , the DEC cannot be necessary or sufficient to rule out acausal propagation of energy-momentum, as there are DEC-violating theories which have a well-defined initial value formulation (limiting their propagation speed to the speed of light) and DEC-satisfying theories which allow signals to propagate superluminally.}
elements.sent-2411-172: In order to interpret the SEC and NEC, the Raychaudhuri equation \eqref{AppA_Raychaudhuri2 , reproduced here for convenience, will prove to be of considerable help: \begin{equation}\frac{d-n}{\eta}\diff{^2\eta}{\lambda^2} = - \sigma_{ab}\sigma^{ab} + \omega_{ab}\omega^{ab} - R_{ab}l^al^b\ . \label{classEC_Raychaudhuri}\end{equation} The Raychaudhuri equation describes, roughly speaking, how the distance between a geodesic with tangent vector $l^a$ and affine parameter $\lambda$ and its neighbours in a congruence evolves.}
elements.sent-2411-173: This distance is characterised by the Jacobi field $\eta$, while $\sigma_{ab}$ and $\omega_{ab}$ are respectively the shear and vorticity of the congruence; finally, $n$ is the codimension of the congruence, i.e. $n=1$ for time- and spacelike congruences and $n=2$ for null congruences.
elements.sent-2411-174: While the shear and vorticity are primarily properties of the congruence, $R_{ab l^al^b$ depends on the curvature of spacetime and is directly constrained by the SEC and NEC.}
elements.sent-2411-175: The SEC posits that $R_{ab t^at^b\geq0$ for any timelike $t^a$.}
elements.sent-2411-176: By \eqref{classEC_Raychaudhuri , this implies that spacetime curvature will draw timelike geodesics together (or at least not push them apart).}
elements.sent-2411-177: However, as \cite{Curiel2017 remarks, $\eta$ is an average of the separation between geodesics that are neighbouring in various directions; the fact that spacetime curvature negatively accelerates $\eta$ does not imply that any two bodies moving on timelike geodesics cannot be accelerated away from each other, only that on average they will not.}
elements.sent-2411-178: The formulation of the SEC in terms of the energy-momentum tensor is less straightforward to interpret: the term constrained by \eqref{classEC_SEC has been interpreted as an effective energy density \cite{EED_Kontou , but it is unclear why it should be constrained.}}
elements.sent-2411-179: Finally, the interpretation of the NEC follows a similar line of reasoning: since the NEC states that $R_{ab l^al^b\geq0$ for all null $l^a$, \eqref{classEC_Raychaudhuri implies that spacetime curvature will not push null geodesics apart.}}
elements.sent-2411-180: Note that this should not be seen as a claim about gravity being attractive, since in a theory which satisfies the NEC but violates the SEC (for example due to the presence of a cosmological constant), gravity could cause timelike geodesics to accelerate away from each other, while causing null geodesics to focus.
elements.sent-2411-181: The formulation in terms of the energy-momentum tensor is, once again, difficult to interpret; analogously to the WEC, we may consider the NEC to be the statement that observers traversing null geodesics must detect positive energy densities, but it is far from obvious what is meant by an observer traversing a null geodesic.
elements.sent-2411-182: Despite the difficulties in interpreting the meaning of the NEC, the rest of this thesis will be concerned with its implications and generalisations, for two reasons.
elements.sent-2411-183: Firstly, we have argued that the NEC is the weakest of the four energy conditions discussed here, so a violation of the NEC immediately implies a violation of all the classical energy conditions.
elements.sent-2411-184: Secondly, it has been argued that violations of the NEC lead to instabilities in the theory \cite{classNEC_stab1,classNEC_stab2,classNEC_stab3 and to violations of e.g. Hawking's area theorem \cite{HawkingA and the generalised second law \cite{classGSL ; constructing a sensible classical theory thus seems to require satisfying the NEC.}}}
elements.sent-2411-185: With this in mind, we explore one particular consequence of the NEC: Penrose's theorem.
elements.sent-2411-186: Fundamentally, Penrose's theorem is not about singularities, for which a universally accepted definition is lacking \cite{Senovilla_1998 ; rather, it is a statement about geodesic completeness.}
elements.sent-2411-187: Beyond the causality-related concepts introduced in section \ref{sec_Causality , the statement and proof of this theorem requires the notion of a trapped surface, which we introduce first.}
elements.sent-2411-188: In a spacetime $M$, consider a codimension 2 spacelike submanifold \T.
elements.sent-2411-189: Since \T\ has codimension 2, it has two normal vectors, one of which is timelike (since \T\ is spacelike); since $g_{ab}$ has Lorentzian signature, the other one is spacelike.
elements.sent-2411-190: If \T\ is orientable, we can choose a consistent orientation for this spacelike normal vector and define two future-directed null congruences that emanate orthogonally from \T, propagating either along or opposite to the spacelike normal vector.
elements.sent-2411-191: For example, if \T\ is a $d-2$ dimensional sphere, these congruences would initially be directed away from the centre of the sphere and towards it.
elements.sent-2411-192: A useful fact about these congruences (which is proven at the end of section \ref{sec_CongRay , or in more detail in \cite{Witten_causality ) is that since they are orthogonal to a codimension 2 spacelike submanifold, $\omega_{ab = 0$ along each of their null geodesics, at least until the geodesic encounters a caustic (a point where $\eta\rightarrow0$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: If we also assume that the NEC holds, the Raychaudhuri equation \eqref{classEC_Raychaudhuri implies that along every geodesic from these two null congruences \begin{equation}\diff{^2\eta}{\lambda^2} = -\frac{\eta}{d-2}\roha{\sigma_{ab}\sigma^{ab} + R_{ab}l^al^b} \leq 0\ , \label{classEC_Penrose1}\end{equation} where the inequality can be imposed because $\eta\geq0$ by definition, $\sigma_{ab}\sigma^{ab}\geq0$ as argued in section \ref{sec_CongRay , and $R_{ab l^al^b\geq0$ as a consequence of the NEC.}}}
elements.sent-2411-194: We can integrate both sides of the inequality in \eqref{classEC_Penrose1 twice to find that the Jacobi field can only grow if it was growing at \T: \begin{equation}\eta(\lambda) \leq \diff{\eta}{\lambda}\biggr\rvert_\T\,\lambda + \eta(0)\ , \label{classEC_JacobiBound}\end{equation} where we assumed that $\lambda=0$ at \T.}
elements.sent-2411-195: Hence, if $\eta$ is initially decreasing on \T, the geodesic must encounter a caustic at a finite affine parameter $\lambda_0$, with \begin{equation}\lambda_0 \leq -\eta(0)\roha{\diff{\eta}{\lambda}\biggr\rvert_\T}^{-1}\ . \label{classEC_CausticAffine}\end{equation} The observation that a future-directed congruence emanating orthogonally from \T\ cannot defocus if it was initially focused, leads one to define a trapped surface \cite{Penrose_1965,HawkingEllis :.}
elements.sent-2411-196: A physical interpretation of a trapped surface is that its presence signals the existence of a region of spacetime where gravity is so strong that even outgoing null geodesics are focused.
elements.sent-2411-197: Penrose's theorem is now concerned with the possibility of a trapped surface in a spacetime which is geodesically complete, obeys any of the energy conditions from section \ref{sec_classEC and hence obeys the NEC, and is globally hyperbolic with a non-compact Cauchy surface $\Sigma$.}
elements.sent-2411-198: Physically, these assumptions imply that there is no edge of spacetime, that matter focuses null geodesics, and that processes throughout spacetime can be predicted based on initial data on $\Sigma$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-199: The exact statement of Penrose's theorem is \cite{Penrose_1965,HawkingEllis,Witten_causality :.}
elements.sent-2411-200: If $M$ satisfies the NEC and the field equations of general relativity (so that $R_{ab l^al^b\geq0$ for all null vectors $l^a$) and contains a trapped surface $\T$, then $M$ cannot be geodesically complete.}
elements.sent-2411-201: To prove this, we first assume that $M$ is geodesically complete.
elements.sent-2411-202: Recall now that $\partial J^+(\T)$ is spanned by prompt curves, which we argued are the segments of null geodesics that emanate orthogonally from \T\ and do not contain caustics.
elements.sent-2411-203: Since these geodesics emanate orthogonally from \T, they are subject to \eqref{classEC_JacobiBound , and because \T\ is a trapped surface, every one of them must encounter a caustic at some finite affine parameter $\lambda_0$, bounded by \eqref{classEC_CausticAffine if we set $\lambda=0$ at \T; the fact that we can extend the geodesics forming $\partial J^+(\T)$ sufficiently far is guaranteed by our assumption of geodesic completeness.}}
elements.sent-2411-204: Since only the part of these geodesics prior to their caustic is prompt, $\partial J^+(\T)$ must be compact.
elements.sent-2411-205: After all, any $p\in\partial J^+(\T)$ can be specified with the spatial orientation of the null geodesic it is on (i.e. whether $p$ is on a geodesic which propagates along or opposite to the spacelike normal vector of \T), the point $q\in\T$ at which this geodesic intersects \T, and the affine parameter $\lambda_p$ assigned to $p$.
elements.sent-2411-206: Since only the part of the geodesic prior to the caustic at $\lambda_0$ is part of $\partial J^+(\T)$, $\lambda_p$ is restricted to a compact interval $0\leq\lambda_p<\lambda_0$, and since $q$ lies on a compact submanifold, $\partial J^+(\T)$ is compact.
elements.sent-2411-207: We now recall that $\partial J^+(\T)$ is an achronal hypersurface.
elements.sent-2411-208: Then, because $M$ is globally hyperbolic with Cauchy surface $\Sigma$, there exists an injective map $\varphi:\partial J^+(\T)\rightarrow\Sigma$; because $\partial J^+(\T)$ is compact, $\varphi\roha{\partial J^+(\T)}$ is compact as well.
elements.sent-2411-209: Since $\Sigma$ is not compact, this compactness implies that $\varphi\roha{\partial J^+(\T)}$ has a boundary in $\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-210: On the other hand, $\partial J^+(\T)$ is a submanifold, and therefore does not have a boundary that can be mapped to a boundary of $\varphi\roha{\partial J^+(\T)}$ in $\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-211: This contradiction means that our starting assumption that $M$ is geodesically complete must have been wrong, completing the proof.
elements.sent-2411-212: We conclude with two remarks regarding theorem \ref{thm_Penrose .}
elements.sent-2411-213: First, as has been pointed out by many authors \cite{HawkingEllis,Senovilla_1998,Penrose_1965,Witten_causality , the argument given here actually proves that the assumptions that a spacetime has a non-compact Cauchy surface, satisfies the NEC and \eqref{H1_EFE , contains a trapped surface, and is geodesically complete are incompatible.}}
elements.sent-2411-214: It does not indicate which assumption fails, and it certainly does not provide sufficient grounds to conclude that a singularity appears, a point which may be illuminated further by looking at de Sitter spacetime \cite{Senovilla_1998,Witten_causality .}
elements.sent-2411-215: This is an exact solution of \eqref{H1_EFE for $T_{ab = -\Lambda g_{ab /(8\pi G_N)$ with $\Lambda\geq0$, which saturates the NEC; assuming $d\geq3$, one can define global coordinates $x^\mu$ and write the metric as \cite{Carroll_2019 \begin{equation}\d s^2 = g_{\mu\nu}\d x^\mu \d x^\nu = -\d t^2 + \alpha^2\cosh^2(t/\alpha)\viha{\d\chi^2 + \sin^2\chi\,\d\Omega_{d-2}^2}\ ,\end{equation} where $\d\Omega_{d-2 ^2$ is the metric of the $d-2$ sphere, $\alpha$ is a constant length scale, and $0\leq\chi\leq\pi$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-216: De Sitter spacetime is globally hyperbolic and geodesically complete, and contains trapped surfaces (e.g. the spacelike $d-2$ spheres at $t<0$); by theorem \ref{thm_Penrose , its Cauchy surface must then be compact, which it is: e.g. $t=0$ defines a Cauchy surface with the topology of a sphere.}
elements.sent-2411-217: In a different set of coordinates $\hat{x ^\mu$, defined on a part of de Sitter spacetime referred to as the (time-reversed) planar patch or flat slicing, the metric can be represented as \cite{HawkingEllis \begin{equation}\d s^2 = g_{\mu\nu}\d \hat{x}^\mu \d \hat{x}^\nu = -\d\hat{t}^2 + e^{-2\hat{t}/\alpha}\d\hat{\ve{x}}^2\ .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-218: This describes a spacetime which has a non-compact Cauchy surface (e.g. defined by $\hat{t = 0$) and contains trapped surfaces (e.g. any spacelike $d-2$ sphere).}
elements.sent-2411-219: It must then be geodesically incomplete by theorem \ref{thm_Penrose , and indeed: once the time-reversed planar patch is embedded in the global de Sitter spacetime, null geodesics can leave it, entering the rest of the global de Sitter spacetime.}
elements.sent-2411-220: Note that the geodesic incompleteness predicted by theorem \ref{thm_Penrose does not imply the appearance of a singularity in this case, but rather of a Cauchy horizon.}
elements.sent-2411-221: Our second and final remark is that the NEC was crucial to the proof of theorem \ref{thm_Penrose : without it, the argument for the compactness of $\partial J^+(\T)$ as given here would not have held and we would not have arrived at a contradiction.}
elements.sent-2411-222: However, the next chapter will show that quantum fields, which describe the matter fields we observe, routinely violate the NEC.
elements.sent-2411-223: Whether the conclusion of Penrose's theorem must be relinquished in a semi-classical context therefore depends on two things: whether any energy condition can be formulated for quantum fields, and if it can be, whether such an energy condition is sufficient to prove a form of Penrose's theorem.
elements.sent-2411-224: Although there has been progress in reformulating Penrose's theorem for weakened energy conditions \cite{weak_thms1,weak_thms2,weak_thms3 , this is beyond the scope of this thesis, and the following chapters will focus on identifying possible energy conditions for quantum fields.}
elements.sent-2411-225: Chapter \ref{chap_ClassEC introduced energy conditions as pointwise restrictions on a contraction of the energy-momentum tensor, and demonstrated their profound implications for the global properties of spacetime by proving Penrose's singularity theorem from the null energy condition (NEC).}
elements.sent-2411-226: In turn, the NEC was argued to be a sensible constraint due to its close relation to the matter theory's stability \cite{classNEC_stab1,classNEC_stab2,classNEC_stab3 and consistency with black hole thermodynamics \cite{HawkingA,classGSL .}}
elements.sent-2411-227: However, these are results of classical field theory.
elements.sent-2411-228: A more realistic description of matter using quantum field theory (QFT) permits negative energy densities, e.g. in the Casimir effect \cite{CasimirEffect , which makes it necessary to reconsider the validity of the NEC if the results of the classical theory are to be maintained in a semi-classical context.}
elements.sent-2411-229: As a first step towards an energy condition for semi-classically self-consistent theories, we will consider QFTs on fixed spacetimes, i.e. we specify the geometry of spacetime without imposing \eqref{H1_SemiclassEFE .}
elements.sent-2411-230: Section \ref{sec_FlatSpacetime considers the simple case of a QFT in Minkowski spacetime.}
elements.sent-2411-231: In this setting, it is shown that the NEC cannot hold as a statement about the expectation value of the energy-momentum tensor (i.e. we cannot demand that $\mean{T_{ab _\psi l^al^b\geq0$ for all null vectors $l^a$ and states $\ket{\psi}$), after which we prove that violations of the NEC cannot be arbitrarily large and long-lasting by arguing that the NEC must hold on average along a complete null geodesic.}}
elements.sent-2411-232: We consider this averaged NEC (ANEC) in some more detail in section \ref{sec_CurvedSpacetimes , where several examples demonstrate that the ANEC cannot hold for QFTs on curved spacetimes.}
elements.sent-2411-233: In this chapter, we consider a QFT with Hilbert space \calH\ and energy-momentum tensor $T_{ab}$.
elements.sent-2411-234: We denote the expectation value of this operator by $\bra{\psi T_{ab}\ket{\psi = \mean{T_{ab _\psi$ for any $\ket{\psi}\in\calH$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-235: A straightforward way to implement the energy conditions \eqref{classEC_WEC -\eqref{classEC_NEC in a QFT is to demand that $\mean{T _\psi \geq 0$ for any $\ket{\psi}\in\calH$, where $T$ is the relevant contraction of $T_{ab}$ (e.g. the NEC for a QFT would have $T = T_{ab l^al^b$ for any null vector $l^a$).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-236: In this section, we consider a QFT in a Minkowski spacetime $M$ and show that it necessarily violates a bound like $\mean{T _\psi\geq0$ if $T$ is a local operator \cite{EGJ_NietPuntsgewijs .}}
elements.sent-2411-237: It is then proven that the average of $\mean{T_{ab _\psi l^al^b$ along a null geodesic with tangent vector $l^a$ will be non-negative \cite{ANEC_Entropy .}}}
elements.sent-2411-238: To prove that energy conditions cannot be formulated as $\mean{T _\psi\geq0$ for a local operator $T$, we require the Reeh-Schlieder theorem \cite{Kontou_review,Haag_1996,Witten_ReehSchlieder (\cite{Reeh_Schlieder_1961 is the German original):.}}}
elements.sent-2411-239: Let $\calH_0\subseteq\calH$ be the set of states that can be created by acting on $\ket{0}$ with a local field operator, and let $\A(\U)$ be the set of local operators of the QFT localised in an open region $\U\subset M$.
elements.sent-2411-240: Then the set $\A(\U)\ket{0}$ is dense in $\calH_0$, i.e. any $\ket{\psi}\in\calH_0$ can be approximated to arbitrary accuracy as $\hat{a}\ket{0}$ for $\hat{a}\in\A(\U)$.
elements.sent-2411-241: An important corollary follows by considering two spacelike separated regions $\U,\U'\subset M$.
elements.sent-2411-242: Due to the spacelike separation, all $\hat{a}\in\A(\U)$ must (anti-)commute with all $\hat{a '\in\A(\U')$ to preserve causality.}
elements.sent-2411-244: However, since $\A(\U')\ket{0}$ is dense in $\calH_0$, this implies that $\hat{a}$ annihilates all $\ket{\psi}\in\calH_0$, which in turn means that $\hat{a}$ must be identically zero.
elements.sent-2411-245: By combining this corollary with the Reeh-Schlieder theorem itself, the following theorem is immediate \cite{Kontou_review,EGJ_NietPuntsgewijs :.}

elements.subsection-2411-008: An information-theoretical proof of the ANEC
elements.sent-2411-255: A loophole presents itself at this point: if we average $T_{ab}$ and its contractions over a non-compact region, the expectation value of this operator may still be bounded.
elements.sent-2411-256: An example of such a bound is the ANEC, which is the statement that for a QFT in Minkowski spacetime \begin{equation}\int_\gamma\d\lambda\, \mean{T_{ab}}_\psi l^al^b \geq0\ . \label{thANEC_ANEC}\end{equation} Here, $\gamma\subset M$ is an inextendible null geodesic with tangent vector $l^a$ and affine parameter $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-257: In order to prove the ANEC quite generally, \cite{ANEC_Entropy uses the monotonicity of the relative entropy.}
elements.sent-2411-258: In this subsection, we will briefly go over the information-theoretical part of their argument, which was also treated in \cite{BlancoCasini_2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-259: The first step is to split the Hilbert space.
elements.sent-2411-260: To that end, consider a Cauchy surface $\Sigma\subset M$; because $\Sigma$ is a Cauchy surface, any state in \calH\ can be reconstructed from the state of the fields on $\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-261: We denote the set of possible states on $\Sigma$ by $\calH_\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-262: This set can be further split up by dividing $\Sigma$ into two regions, $A$ and $A^c$, such that $A\cup A^c=\Sigma$ and $A\cap A^c = \emptyset$; if the QFT is local, any state on $\Sigma$ can be split into a state on $A$ and a state on $A^c$, since these regions are spacelike separated.
elements.sent-2411-264: As the next step, we recall from section \ref{sec_DensityMatrices that a density matrix can be assigned to the field configuration on $\Sigma$ as \begin{equation}\rho^\psi = \sum_\alpha c^\psi_\alpha\smallket{\psi_\alpha}\smallbra{\psi_\alpha}\ ,\end{equation} where $\ket{\psi_\alpha}\in\calH_\Sigma$ are unit vectors, and the $c^\psi_\alpha$ are restricted by $0\leq c^\psi_\alpha\leq1$ and $\sum_\alpha c^\psi_\alpha = 1$ \cite{Griffiths_QM .}}
elements.sent-2411-265: Because $\calH_\Sigma$ factorises, we can define a reduced density matrix $\rho^\psi_A$ on $A$, as \begin{equation}\rho^\psi_A = \Tr_{A^c}(\rho^\psi) = \sum_\alpha c^\psi_\alpha\smallket{\psi^A_\alpha}\smallbra{\psi^A_\alpha}\ ,\end{equation} where $\Tr_{A^c}$ is defined in \eqref{AppB_ReducedDensity and the $\smallket{\psi^A_\alpha}\in\calH_A$ are again unit vectors.}
elements.sent-2411-266: With the reduced density matrix, expectation values of operators $\order_A$ that act only on the field configuration on $A$ can be denoted as $\mean{\order_A _\psi = \Tr_A(\rho^\psi_A\order_A)$.}
elements.sent-2411-267: Furthermore, it can be used to define two quantities of great importance to quantum information theory.
elements.sent-2411-268: The first is the entanglement entropy across the entangling surface $\partial A$: \begin{equation}S_e[\psi,A] = -\Tr_A(\rho^\psi_A\ln\rho^\psi_A)\ . \label{thANEC_EntanglementEntropy}\end{equation} The other quantity is the modular Hamiltonian: \begin{equation}K^\psi_A = -\ln\rho^\psi_A\ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-269: Of course, a similar modular Hamiltonian $K^\psi_{A^c}$ can be defined for the field configuration on $A^c$, and they can be combined into the full modular Hamiltonian as \begin{equation}\hat{K}^\psi_A = K^\psi_A\otimes1_{A^c} - 1_A\otimes K^\psi_{A^c}\ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-270: Both the entanglement entropy and modular Hamiltonian play a role in the relative entropy.
elements.sent-2411-271:
This quantity, which indicates the degree to which states $\ket{\psi ,\ket{\phi}\in\calH_\Sigma$ can be distinguished based on measurements restricted to $A$, is defined as  egin{equation}S(ho^\psi_A|ho^\phi_A) \equiv \ \Tr_A(ho^\psi_A\lnho^\psi_A) - \Tr_A(ho^\psi_A\lnho^\phi_A) 
on \alis \Tr_A(ho^\psi_A\lnho^\psi_A) - \Tr_A(ho^\phi_A\lnho^\phi_A) -\Tr_A(ho^\phi_AK^\phi_A) + \Tr_A(ho^\psi_A K^\phi_A)

on \equiv \ -\Delta S_e[A] + \Delta \smallmean{K^\phi_A}\ . \label{thANEC_RelEnt}\end{equation} The relative entropy has two important properties: it is non-negative and monotonous \cite{Witten_ReehSchlieder .}}
elements.sent-2411-272: The latter property is the statement that \begin{equation}S(\rho^\psi_a|\rho^\phi_a) \leq S(\rho^\psi_A|\rho^\phi_A)\ , \label{thANEC_RelEntMon}\end{equation} if we choose a region $a$ such that $D(a)\subset D(A)$; in essence, this property means that it is more difficult to distinguish states by taking measurements in a smaller region.
elements.sent-2411-273: By combining \eqref{thANEC_RelEnt and \eqref{thANEC_RelEntMon , it follows that \begin{equation}\Delta \smallmean{K^\phi_a} - \Delta \smallmean{K^\phi_A} + \Delta S_e[A] - \Delta S_e[a] \leq 0\ . \label{thANEC_ApplyMonotonic1}\end{equation} In particular, choose a second Cauchy surface $\Sigma'$ with $a\subset\Sigma'$, and call $a^c=\Sigma'\setminus a$.}}
elements.sent-2411-274: The equivalent of \eqref{thANEC_ApplyMonotonic1 for $a^c$ and $A^c$ must take into account that $D(A^c)\subset D(a^c)$, leading to \begin{equation}\Delta \smallmean{K^\phi_{A^c}} - \Delta \smallmean{K^\phi_{a^c}} + \Delta S_e[a^c] - \Delta S_e[A^c] \leq 0\ . \label{thANEC_ApplyMonotonic2}\end{equation} Adding \eqref{thANEC_ApplyMonotonic1 and \eqref{thANEC_ApplyMonotonic2 together yields \begin{equation}\Delta\smallmean{\hat{K}^\phi_a} - \Delta\smallmean{\hat{K}^\phi_A} + \Delta S_e[A] - \Delta S_e[A^c] - \Delta S_e[a] + \Delta S_e[a^c] \leq 0\ . \label{thANEC_ApplyMonotonic3}\end{equation} The final step taken in \cite{ANEC_Entropy is to choose two specific field configurations; following them, we choose $\phi$ to be the vacuum $\ket{0}$ and let $\psi$ denote a pure state $\ket{\psi}\in\calH_\Sigma$ ($\ket{\psi}$ can then be evolved to $\Sigma'$ through the equations of motion to obtain the corresponding state in $\calH_{\Sigma'}$).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-275: Since these are both pure states, all terms in \eqref{thANEC_ApplyMonotonic3 that involve the entanglement entropy must vanish.}
elements.sent-2411-276: To see this, consider for example $S_e[\psi,A]$ for a pure state $\ket{\psi}\in\calH_\Sigma$; the other terms can be treated similarly.
elements.sent-2411-277: The reduced density matrix for this state is \begin{equation}\rho^\psi_A = \Tr_{A^c}(\rho^\psi) = \sum_i\langle\psi^{A^c}_i|\psi\rangle\langle\psi|\psi^{A^c}_i\rangle = \smallket{\psi^A}\smallbra{\psi^A}\ ,\end{equation} since $\ket{\psi = \smallket{\psi^A}\smallket{\psi^{A^c }$ with $\smallket{\psi^{A }\in\calH_{A}$ and $\smallket{\psi^{A^c }\in\calH_{A^c}$ both unit vectors.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-278: Importantly, $\rho^\psi_A$ also describes a pure state, which means that $(\rho^\psi_A)^2 = \rho^\psi_A$.
elements.sent-2411-279: We can then evaluate the following quantity (using a trick from \cite{Kiritsis ): \begin{equation}\rho^\psi_A\ln\rho^\psi_A = \lim_{n\rightarrow1}\parti{(\rho^\psi_A)^n}{n} = \lim_{n\rightarrow1}\parti{\rho^\psi_A}{n} = 0\ .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-280: Hence, the entanglement entropy of a pure state vanishes, leaving only the terms involving modular Hamiltonians in \eqref{thANEC_ApplyMonotonic3 .}
elements.sent-2411-281: Since the full modular Hamiltonian annihilates the vacuum \cite{BlancoCasini_2013 , \eqref{thANEC_ApplyMonotonic3 reduces to \begin{equation}\smallmean{\hat{K}^0_A}_\psi - \smallmean{\hat{K}^0_a}_\psi \geq 0\ , \label{thANEC_ModHamPos}\end{equation} where the superscript indicates that this is the modular Hamiltonian of the vacuum.}}
elements.sent-2411-282: It is difficult to calculate these modular Hamiltonians in general, but the difference in \eqref{thANEC_ModHamPos can be calculated perturbatively if $a$ is an infinitesimal deformation of $A$.}
elements.sent-2411-283: If one chooses $A = \cuha{x\in M:x^0 = 0,\,x^1>0}$ and picks an infinitesimal vector field $\zeta^a$ with, in null coordinates, $\zeta^\mu = (\zeta^+,\zeta^-,\ve{0 ^\perp) = \zeta^\mu(\ve{x ^\perp)$ and $\pm\zeta^\pm>0$, then \cite{ANEC_Entropy proved that \begin{equation}\smallmean{\hat{K}^0_A}_\psi - \smallmean{\hat{K}^0_a}_\psi = 2\pi\int\d^{d-2}\ve{x}^\perp\roha{\zeta^+\int_{L_+(\ve{x}^\perp)}\d x^+\,\smallmean{T_{++}}_\psi - \zeta^-\int_{L_-(\ve{x}^\perp)}\d x^-\,\smallmean{T_{--}}_\psi}\ . \label{thANEC_ModHamANEC}\end{equation} In this expression, $L_+(\ve{x ^\perp)$ is a complete null geodesic with affine parameter $\lambda$ such that $L_+(\ve{x ^\perp) = \cuha{x\in M:x^\mu = (x^+ = \lambda,x^- = 0,\ve{x ^\perp)}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-284: Similarly, the coordinates along $L_-(\ve{x ^\perp)$ can be written as $x^\mu(\lambda) = (x^+=0,x^-=\lambda,\ve{x ^\perp)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-286: The ANEC \eqref{thANEC_ANEC follows because this should hold for any choice of $\zeta^+ = \zeta^+(\ve{x ^\perp)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-287: In the previous section, it was shown that the ANEC holds for general QFTs in Minkowski spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-288: The situation is more complicated in other spacetimes, so in this section several examples of violations of the ANEC will be given.
elements.sent-2411-289: A first counterexample to the ANEC can be given in a Minkowski spacetime in which one spatial direction is compactified \cite{Klinkhammer .}
elements.sent-2411-290: As a result, a phenomenon closely related to the Casimir effect \cite{CasimirEffect occurs, and the ANEC is violated on any null geodesic that moves along the compactified direction.}
elements.sent-2411-291: Another class of counterexamples can be found for QFTs that would be conformally invariant field theories (CFTs) in flat spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-292: These theories are invariant under Weyl transformations \cite{WeylVsConformal , that is, local rescalings of the metric as given in \eqref{AppA_Weyl : \begin{equation}\tilde{g}_{ab}(x)\rightarrow g_{ab}(x) = \Omega^2(x)\tilde{g}_{ab}(x) \ ,\end{equation} for a smooth, non-zero function $\Omega:M\rightarrow\mathbb{R}$ called the conformal factor.}}
elements.sent-2411-293: If $\tilde{T _{ab}$ and $T_{ab}$ are the energy-momentum tensors of the CFT in the original and transformed spacetime respectively, they are related as in \eqref{AppB_CFTEMT : \begin{equation}U\tilde{T}_{ab}U^\dagger = \Omega^{d-2}(x)\roha{T_{ab} - X_{ab}}\ , \label{thANEC_TransformEMT}\end{equation} where $U:\tilde{\calH}\rightarrow\calH$ is a unitary map between states in the original and transformed spacetime, and $X_{ab}$ is a purely geometrical conformal anomaly \cite{Conforme_anomalie .}}}
elements.sent-2411-294: We may choose $\tilde{g _{ab = \eta_{ab}$; then the ANEC integral in the transformed spacetime can be related to an integral in Minkowski spacetime, since \begin{equation}\int_\gamma \mean{T_{ab}}_\psi l^al^b\d\lambda = \int_\gamma \Omega^{-d}(x)\smallmean{\tilde{T}_{ab}}_{\tilde{\psi}}\tilde{l}^a\tilde{l}^b + X_{ab}\tilde{l}^a\tilde{l}^b\,\d\tilde{\lambda}\ , \label{thANEC_TransformANEC}\end{equation} where we used the fact that Weyl transformations leave null geodesics invariant, defined $\smallket{\tilde{\psi = U^\dagger\ket{\psi}$, and used \eqref{AppA_WeylAffine .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-295: It may happen that this integral is still non-negative; this is the case for e.g. (anti-)de Sitter spacetime, for which $\Omega$ is constant along $\gamma$ and $X_{ab}\tilde{l ^a\tilde{l ^b=0$ \cite{Rosso_2020 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-296: In these instances, the ANEC continues to hold, but for most choices of $\Omega$, \eqref{thANEC_TransformANEC indicates that the ANEC can be violated in two distinct ways: the conformal factor $\Omega$ can enhance the contribution of a segment of $\gamma$ along which the null energy condition is violated, or the conformal anomaly violates the ANEC \cite{ANEC_violation_old,ANEC_violation_new,ANEC_violation_VeryOld .}}
elements.sent-2411-297: Finally, one may consider spacetimes that are unrelated to Minkowski spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-298: An example of this is provided by a QFT in Schwarzschild spacetime, where the conformally coupled (but otherwise free) massless scalar field violates the ANEC for any null geodesic that does not cross the event horizon if one considers the Boulware vacuum \cite{ANECviolation_Schwarzschild .}
elements.sent-2411-299: This state, which is the vacuum as seen by a stationary observer outside the event horizon, is singular at the event horizon \cite{Carroll_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-300: This may indicate that it is not physically relevant.
elements.sent-2411-301: Nevertheless, the ANEC is violated on null geodesics that never cross the event horizon, and thus it should be possible to violate the ANEC with a state that agrees with the Boulware vacuum well outside the black hole but smooths out the divergence.
elements.sent-2411-302: In summary, section \ref{sec_FlatSpacetime showed that QFTs routinely violate the pointwise null energy condition and this section demonstrated that they also violate the averaged version when considered in curved spacetimes.}
elements.sent-2411-303: However, the success of the ANEC in Minkowski spacetime hints at the possibility of a generally valid energy condition.
elements.sent-2411-304: For this, we would have to modify the ANEC to a form suitable for various geometries.
elements.sent-2411-305: Two approaches to this modification are discussed in the next chapter: the conformally invariant ANEC and the self-consistent achronal ANEC.
elements.sent-2411-306: As the previous chapter showed, quantum field theories (QFTs) in Minkowski spacetime obey the averaged null energy condition (ANEC), which states that \begin{equation}\int_\gamma\d\lambda\,\mean{T_{ab}}_\psi l^al^b\geq0\end{equation} for any complete null geodesic $\gamma$ with affine parameter $\lambda$ and tangent vector $l^a$ and any state $\ket{\psi}$.
elements.sent-2411-307: However, several examples showed that QFTs fail to obey this condition when considered in curved spacetimes, which is a relevant setting if one is interested in the role of energy conditions in (semi-classical) gravity.
elements.sent-2411-308: This chapter therefore examines two generalisations of the ANEC to curved spacetimes.
elements.sent-2411-309: To facilitate this discussion, section \ref{sec_AdS/CFT very briefly introduces the AdS/CFT correspondence, which is used in section \ref{sec_CANEC to prove, for holographic field theories in a class of fixed curved spacetimes, the $d=3$ conformally invariant ANEC \eqref{intro_CANEC or CANEC.}}}
elements.sent-2411-310: Although this condition is secure against violations by Weyl transformations, its proof only holds for a relatively restricted range of situations.
elements.sent-2411-311: Thus, section \ref{sec_AANEC goes beyond the framework of QFT on fixed spacetime and considers a geometry determined by the quantum field.}
elements.sent-2411-312: A perturbative proof by Wall \cite{Wall of the resulting self-consistent achronal ANEC (AANEC) is presented.}
elements.sent-2411-313: As chapter \ref{chap_Intro remarked, general relativity is a classical theory, while realistic matter and its interactions are described by QFTs.}
elements.sent-2411-314: Often, this discrepancy can be dealt with using semi-classical gravity \cite{Rosenfeld_1963 , but this is not a quantum theory of gravity, which remains elusive.}
elements.sent-2411-315: An example of progress towards such a theory is the AdS/CFT correspondence, first proposed in \cite{Maldacena , which we introduce very briefly in this section; a more extensive introduction can be found in e.g. \cite{Kiritsis or \cite{Polchinski_AdS/CFT and references therein.}}}
elements.sent-2411-316: Anti-de Sitter (AdS) spacetime is the maximally symmetric spacetime with constant Ricci scalar $R < 0$ (i.e. the AdS metric is invariant under the maximum number of independent diffeomorphisms and the AdS manifold is negatively curved); as such, it contains no matter and has a negative cosmological constant $\Lambda < 0$.
elements.sent-2411-317: In coordinates that cover an entire $d+1$ dimensional AdS manifold, the line element is \begin{equation}\d s^2 = \roha{-\cosh^2(\rho/L)\d t^2 + \d\rho^2 + L^2\sinh^2(\rho/L)\d\Omega_{d-1}^2}\ , \label{curveANEC_AdSGlobal}\end{equation} where $t\inR$ and $\rho\geq0$, $L$ is a length scale related to $\Lambda$ as $\Lambda = -d(d-1)/(2L^2)$, and $\d\Omega^2_{d-1}$ is the metric of the $d-1$ dimensional unit sphere.
elements.sent-2411-318: An important property of AdS spacetime is that it has a timelike conformal boundary, which becomes apparent upon defining new coordinates $\chi$ and $\tau$ with $\cos\chi = 1/\cosh(\rho/L)$ and $\tau = t/L$, so that the metric reads \begin{equation}\d s^2 = \frac{L^2}{\cos^2\chi}\roha{-\d \tau^2 + \d\chi^2 + \sin^2\chi\,\d\Omega_{d-1}^2}\ . \label{curveANEC_AdSSphere}\end{equation} This shows that AdS spacetime is conformally related to the Einstein static universe (which has the geometry described by the line element in brackets), restricted to $0\leq\chi<\frac{\pi {2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-319: Therefore, AdS spacetime has a timelike boundary at $\chi = \frac{\pi {2}$ and is not globally hyperbolic, since null geodesics can terminate on the boundary in finite coordinate time.}
elements.sent-2411-320: If a spacetime $M$ has $\Lambda < 0$ and contains matter fields that decay sufficiently quickly towards the conformal boundary $\partial M$, then the causal structure of $M$ asymptotically approaches that of AdS spacetime near $\partial M$.

elements.sent-2411-331: Another important consequence of the AdS/CFT correspondence is that signals between points $p,q\in\partial M$ may travel on two types of curves: those that stay in the boundary, and those that move into the bulk.
elements.sent-2411-332: If the boundary CFT is a sensible theory on its own, it should not allow signals to travel between points that cannot be connected by a causal curve in the boundary.
elements.sent-2411-333: It makes sense, then, to demand that there is no causal bulk curve connecting points in $\partial M$ that cannot be causally connected by a boundary curve.
elements.sent-2411-334: A spacetime $M\cup\partial M$ with this property is said to have the no-bulk-shortcut property.
elements.sent-2411-335: Following \cite{CANEC_odd , we will use this correspondence and the related no-bulk-shortcut property to demonstrate that, at least for $d=3$, holographic CFTs obey the conformally invariant ANEC (CANEC).}
elements.sent-2411-336: The main idea of this proof of CANEC is that we can choose two points in $\partial M$ that cannot be connected by a causal boundary curve, and then attempt to construct a causal bulk curve which does connect them.
elements.sent-2411-337: Insisting on the no-bulk-shortcut property leads to the CANEC.
elements.sent-2411-338: We begin with general $d$ and choose a point $p\in\partial M$.
elements.sent-2411-339: Now let $\partial J^+(p)\subset\partial M$ be the boundary of the causal future of $p$ in $\partial M$, and choose $q\in \partial J^+(p)$; this means that there is a null geodesic $\gamma\subset\partial M$ connecting $p$ to $q$.
elements.sent-2411-340: Assuming $d\geq3$, we can choose a codimension 2 spacelike submanifold $W\subset\partial M$ such that $\gamma$ intersects $W$ orthogonally in $p$.
elements.sent-2411-341: This submanifold can be used to choose Gaussian normal coordinates $x^\mu = (u,v,x^I)$, with $I\in\cuha{2,d-1}$, as the boundary coordinates $x^\mu$ in a region containing $p$ and $q$.
elements.sent-2411-342: These coordinates are described in more detail in section \ref{sec_CongRay (based on \cite{Witten_causality ), but their most important feature is that curves with fixed $v$ and $x^I$ describe null geodesics with affine parameter $u$ that emanate orthogonally from $W$.}}
